data.title: HHS sends proposed drug pricing rule to White House for review
data.description:
The White House announced on Thursday that a proposed drug pricing rule drafted by the Department of Health & Human Services (HHS) and the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) is currently pending its review.

An economically significant proposed rule titled “Global Benchmark for Efficient Drug Pricing (GLOBE) Model” has been received by the White House’s Office of Management and Budget, according to a notice on the agency’s website.

The development marks the U.S. government’s latest effort to lower U.S. drug prices to the level of other developed nations in accordance with President Donald Trump’s most-favored-nation (MFN) pricing policy.

In May, Trump signed an executive order reviving the rule, which he initially introduced in 2020 but was later blocked by federal courts and rescinded by the then-Biden administration.

The proposed rule comes days ahead of a Sept. 29 deadline set by Trump for major pharmaceutical companies to lower U.S. drug prices in line with the MFN policy. He sends letters to the CEOs of 17 leading drugmakers, including Eli Lilly (LLY), Pfizer (NYSE:PFE), and Merck (NYSE:MRK), issuing a 60-day ultimatum to comply with the rule.

Other U.S. pharma majors to receive the letters included Gilead (GILD), Bristol Myers (BMY), J&J (NYSE:JNJ), Regeneron (REGN), Amgen (AMGN), and AbbVie (ABBV). European drugmakers Merck KGaA (OTCPK:MKGAF), Sanofi (SNY), GSK (NYSE:GSK), AstraZeneca (AZN), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY), and Novartis (NVS) were also part of the group.

data.title: Jabil outlines 25% AI revenue growth for FY '26 as North Carolina facility ramps
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Jabil Inc. (JBL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Michael Meheryar Dastoor stated fiscal 2025 was “a great year for Jabil,” highlighting solid revenue growth, core operating margin expansion, and strong free cash flow. He emphasized, “FY '25 demonstrated 2 things at once. Some end markets were choppy, yet the company remained resilient, where automotive and renewables slowed, AI-driven demand more than offset where consumer categories soften, CLDC expanded margins through portfolio pruning. And across the enterprise, free cash flow came in extremely strong.”

  • CFO Gregory Hebard reported, “For the fourth quarter, our team delivered strong performance, reaching approximately $8.3 billion in revenue, which exceeded the midpoint of our guidance by roughly $800 million... Core operating income for the quarter came in at $519 million, well above the high end of our expected range.” Hebard also noted, “During the fourth quarter, we completed our prior $1 billion share repurchase authorization...In July, our Board authorized a new $1 billion share repurchase program, giving us continued flexibility to return capital thoughtfully and opportunistically, we intend to fully execute the current authorization in fiscal '26.”

  • EVP Steve Borges described the regulated industries segment as “thoughtful and deliberate,” focusing on “software-defined vehicles, advanced driver assistance systems, compute, and other powertrain agnostic solutions” in automotive, and highlighting new wins in health care with an emphasis on biologics, GLP-1s, and injectables.

  • EVP Matt Crowley explained, “Our strategy is to engage at the rack scale level. Instead of selling individual parts, we're integrating full racks into one deliverable system. That's a huge differentiator.” Crowley also emphasized investment in liquid cooling, advanced power integration, and system-level solutions for cloud and data center infrastructure.

Outlook

  • Hebard provided guidance for Q1 FY '26: “total company revenue for Q1 is expected to be in the range of $7.7 billion to $8.3 billion. Core operating income for Q1 is estimated to be in the range of $400 million to $460 million... Core diluted earnings per share is estimated to be in the range of $2.47 to $2.87.”

  • Dastoor projected, “For FY '26, we expect approximately 5% revenue growth to about $31.3 billion. We expect core operating margin to expand by roughly 20 basis points to around 5.6%... Core earnings per share is expected to be $11 in FY '26, and we anticipate free cash flow to be greater than $1.3 billion.”

  • AI-related revenue is forecast to grow 25% in FY '26, reaching about $11.2 billion, with the new North Carolina facility expected to support capacity for future years.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue reached $8.3 billion, with core operating income of $519 million and core operating margin at 6.3%. Net interest expense for Q4 was $65 million. GAAP operating income was $337 million, and diluted EPS was $1.99. Core diluted EPS was $3.29.

  • By segment: Regulated Industries revenue was $3.1 billion, Intelligent Infrastructure $3.7 billion, and Connected Living & Digital Commerce $1.4 billion. Cash from operations for Q4 was $588 million, with full-year adjusted free cash flow of more than $1.3 billion.

  • Inventory days improved to 69, and net inventory days were 55. Debt to core EBITDA was 1.3x, with cash balances at approximately $1.9 billion.

Q&A

  • Ruplu Bhattacharya, BofA Securities, Research Division: Asked about AI segment growth and market share. Matt Crowley responded, “We're growing 25% in our AI revenue year-on-year. So going from $9 billion to $11.2 billion. ...we feel like we're gaining share, especially in the data center infrastructure business.”

  • Bhattacharya asked about health care growth and the Croatia facility. Steven Borges confirmed “Equation is on track as planned. There has been no changes. There are no delays.” Dastoor clarified, “Croatia was never meant to be an FY '26 event. It was always in FY '27... and that is exactly how it's turning out.”

  • Mark Delaney, Goldman Sachs: Questioned data center capacity and margins. Crowley said, “We're going to continue to operate sites at 24/7... we're retrofitting the sites that exist to be prepared for liquid cooling.” Mark Mondello explained, “where we have invested in specific capabilities like our silicon photonics business, like our data center infrastructure business, we expect margins to be accretive.”

  • Steven Fox, Fox Advisors LLC: Asked about margin guidance and product pruning. Hebard answered, “we do see a 20 basis point pickup in our margins from 26 to 25... We still have some headwinds on unutilized capacity outside of the U.S.” Andy Priestley added, “We have made a conscious decision... a deliberate shift away from some of the lower margin and commoditized products towards more of an engineering-led and margin-accretive product.”

  • Melissa Fairbanks, Raymond James: Asked about potential tariff impacts in health care and capacity utilization. Borges said, “our health care business and our auto business for that matter is region for region consumption. So we're in a good place from a manufacturing standpoint.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was focused on AI growth, capacity utilization, margin expansion, and potential geopolitical/tariff risks, reflecting a neutral-to-slightly-positive outlook as analysts pressed for detail on growth drivers and risk mitigation.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing execution, resilience, and a deliberate portfolio shift. Dastoor frequently highlighted “conservative” and “prudent” guidance, and Crowley and Borges reiterated confidence in demand and growth trajectories.

  • Compared to last quarter, both analysts and management showed sustained optimism around AI and Intelligent Infrastructure, with continued conservatism in regulated and consumer segments. Management’s confidence in AI-related growth was more pronounced, while acknowledging ongoing risks in auto and renewables.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for FY '26 represents an increase in revenue growth and margin targets versus the previous quarter, with clear emphasis on AI segment expansion and capital allocation discipline.

  • Strategic focus has shifted further toward AI-related system integration, with the North Carolina facility highlighted as a major capacity enabler for future years.

  • Analysts continued to probe on capacity, margin headwinds, and the impact of macro/tariff issues, consistent with the cautious tone from the prior quarter, but with more focus on execution and fill rates for new facilities.

  • Management reiterated the path to 6% core operating margins, with improvement in utilization and mix as primary drivers, and maintained a more assertive stance on portfolio pruning and margin accretion in Connected Living & Digital Commerce.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged ongoing softness in automotive and renewables, with Dastoor stating, “the team remained resilient, where automotive and renewables slowed, AI-driven demand more than offset.”

  • Portfolio pruning and underutilized capacity outside the U.S. remain margin headwinds, but Hebard noted, “we still have some headwinds on unutilized capacity outside of the U.S. So that's still a 20 to 25 basis points of headwind for us.”

  • Geopolitical and tariff risks are being managed through regional manufacturing and supply chain orchestration, with Borges noting positive positioning for potential pharmaceutical tariff changes.

Final Takeaway

Jabil’s management highlighted a year of strong execution, led by substantial growth in AI-related Intelligent Infrastructure and disciplined portfolio management. The company outlined expectations for 25% AI revenue growth in FY '26, continued margin expansion, and robust free cash flow. Strategic investments in system-level integration, automation, and new U.S. capacity are intended to position Jabil for sustained double-digit growth in key segments, while prudent risk management and a focus on higher-margin opportunities underpin the company’s confidence in delivering value to shareholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oracle, Silver Lake, and MGX to own ~45% of TikTok U.S. - CNBC
data.description:
Software giant Oracle (NYSE:ORCL), private equity firm Silver Lake, and Abu Dhabi-based fund manager MGX will have majority control of TikTok's U.S. business, CNBC reported Thursday, citing sources.

The trio will own about a 45% stake in TikTok U.S., Beijing-based ByteDance (BDNCE) will have a 19.9% stake, and the remaining 35% will be held by new and existing investors, according to the CNBC report.

Sources told CNBC that backers of ByteDance, including General Atlantic, Sequoia, and Susquehanna, are expected to contribute equity in TikTok U.S. 

Susquehanna, owned by billionaire Republican megadonor Jeff Yass, is already a major ByteDance investor. Yass also owns a stake in Trump's social media platform, Truth Social (DJT).

White House officials on Wednesday confirmed that China has agreed to the conditions laid out by the U.S. and that all parties involved in the deal have also accepted the terms. President Donald Trump is expected to sign the long-awaited deal on Thursday to keep the app running for over 170M users in the country. 

Since his re-election, Trump has held a "soft spot" for the app and has partially credited TikTok for his election victory against Democratic candidate Kamala Harris.

Trump has signed four executive orders since taking office in January to delay a bipartisan law passed during the Biden administration that requires the app to be sold to a U.S. entity due to national security concerns.

data.title: Oklo retreats from record highs on wave of insider selling, Goldman analyst caution
data.description:
Oklo (NYSE:OKLO) -9.7% in Thursday's trading, pulling back from a dizzying rally that recently doubled the share price in two weeks, whacked by a batch of insider selling over the past few days and a downbeat report from Goldman Sachs that sees the stock pulling back over the next 12 months.

Oklo (NYSE:OKLO) CEO Jacob DeWitte unloaded $3 million of stock in the form of a gift this week, board member Michael Klein sold $6.7 million worth of shares, and CFO Craig Bealmear sold $9.4 million of shares at the end of last week, according to data from Verity.

Goldman Sachs initiated coverage of Oklo (OKLO) with a Neutral rating and $117 price target, with analyst Brian Lee noting the company has no revenue and likely will not start earning any until 2028, adding seeing less than $300 million in sales even as far out as 2031, making it difficult to support the stock's $20 billion valuation.

"Importantly, Oklo has secured one of the largest backlogs among its small modular reactor peers, with a pipeline of deals totaling more than 14 GW," Lee wrote, but the company "has yet to secure a finalized power purchase agreement with any customers."

Oklo (OKLO) "has been a catalyst-driven stock, and while we see a path for continued near-term catalysts, we believe the company needs to secure finalized customer agreements," Lee said, adding that the company's business model of owning and operating its power plants is a "heavy capital burden" that presents a "meaningful risk to Oklo's success."

data.title: Freddie Mac announces ~$487M non-performing loans sale
data.description:
Freddie Mac (OTCQB:FMCC) announced on Thursday a sale via auction of ~$487M worth of non-performing loans.

The seasoned, deeply delinquent residential first lien whole loans are being marketed via five pools: four Standard Pool Offerings and one Extended Timeline Pool Offering.

Bids are due by October 16 for the SPO pools and by October 30 for the EXPO pool.

data.title: Chewy upgraded as MoffettNathanson bets on the pet industry's underdog
data.description:
After five years of post-COVID volatility, pet-owning households are expected to increase by 1% each year through 2027, leaving pure play Chewy (NYSE:CHWY) positioned to capture a large chunk of the pet wallet.

Betting on the “underdog” in the pet industry, MoffettNathanson raised its outlook on Chewy (NYSE:CHWY) to Buy from Neutral and raised its price target by 45% to $48.

“Our conviction is grounded in a refreshed industry model for pet household formation,” said MoffettNathanson analyst Michael Morton, and with retail giants all battling for their bite of ~$1,700 in annual spending per pet, Chewy has shown a “remarkable ability to consistently count roughly one-third of pet-owning households as active customers.” A trend Morton expects to continue through 2027, with Chewy’s (NYSE:CHWY) multiple likely to benefit as a result.

“In a normalized gross adds environment, we expect Chewy to capture between 2M to 3M gross adds, representing ~30% of industry gross ads. Despite operating in an increasingly competitive vertical, Chewy continues to execute its way towards pet household capture,” Morton adds.

And as each successive generation appears to spend more on pets than the preceding one, powerful trends favorable to Chewy (CHWY) show no signs of slowing down.

While margins and customer additions will be “bumpy” in the near term, Morton sees a healthy business that will deepen its relationship with pet parents over the long term, driving “attractive economics” for Chewy.

MoffetNathanson’s Buy rating conforms with other Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors who also rate the online pet retailer as a Buy. Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating, however, views Chewy (CHWY) as a Hold with a Quant score of 2.84 out of 5.

data.title: Tech Voices: Amazon-FTC settlement, xAI sues OpenAI, EC's SAP probe
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, and remarks that could impact the technology sector.

  • The FTC said Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) will pay $2.5 billion to settle claims that it engaged in deceptive business practices with its Prime subscription service.

Under the agreement, Amazon will pay a $1 billion civil penalty and refund $1.5 billion to around 35 million customers who were impacted by unwanted Prime enrollment or deferred cancellation. The tech giant has also agreed to cease certain business practices regarding Prime enrollment and cancellation. 

“Today, the Trump-Vance FTC made history and secured a record-breaking, monumental win for the millions of Americans who are tired of deceptive subscriptions that feel impossible to cancel,” FTC Chairman Andrew Ferguson said in a statement. 

“The evidence showed that Amazon used sophisticated subscription traps designed to manipulate consumers into enrolling in Prime, and then made it exceedingly hard for consumers to end their subscription. Today, we are putting billions of dollars back into Americans’ pockets, and making sure Amazon never does this again," Ferguson added.

  • AI developer xAI is suing OpenAI for allegedly stealing trade secrets.

In a lawsuit filed Wednesday, xAI asserted that OpenAI has been hiring former xAI employees to gain access to its trade secrets.

"OpenAI is targeting those individuals with knowledge of xAI’s key technologies and business plans—including xAI’s source code and its operational advantages in launching data centers—then inducing those employees to breach their confidentiality and other obligations to xAI through unlawful means," the lawsuit said, according to Reuters.

Tesla (TSLA) CEO Elon Musk controls xAI and has been bullish about using its AI technology in Tesla vehicles and robots. Tesla shareholders are scheduled to vote in November on whether to allow Tesla (TSLA) to invest in xAI.

Musk is also a co-founder of OpenAI, which counts Microsoft (MSFT) as a major shareholder. 

  • The European Commission said Thursday that it is launching an investigation into SAP's (NYSE:SAP) software support services practices.

“Thousands of companies across Europe use SAP’s software to run their business, as well as its related maintenance and support services,” the EC's Teresa Ribera said in a statement. “We are concerned that SAP may have restricted competition in this crucial aftermarket by making it harder for rivals to compete, leaving European customers with fewer choices and higher costs.”

  • SAP (NYSE:SAP) confirmed Thursday that the EC had started "formal proceedings" involving the company's on-premise maintenance and support policies.

"SAP believes that its policies and actions are fully in line with competition rules. However, we take the issues raised seriously, and we are working closely with the EU Commission to resolve them," the company said in a statement.

"We do not anticipate the engagement with the European Commission to result in material impacts on our financial performance," SAP added.

data.title: All about Toyota's very own mind-boggling futuristic city in the foothills of Mount Fuji
data.description:
Toyota (NYSE:TM) launched the first phase of its concept for a futuristic smart city brimming with tech innovations. Woven City is an ambitious real-world test course for mobility and smart city technologies. The name Woven City is an indicator that the pathways toward the advancement of next-generation technology are cleanly woven into the city.

Located at the base of Mount Fuji in Susono City, Japan, the project aims to foster groundbreaking innovations in how people, goods, information, and energy move and interact, serving both as a living laboratory and a prototype for cities of the future.

The city will initially have 100 residents, who will be mainly Toyota (NYSE:TM) and Woven employees. The goal is for a first-phase population of 360 to create a blueprint for a town of 2,000 people. The city is only partially accessible to the public until at least late 2026.

 

 

 

 

"Through our AD/ADAS technology, our software development platform Arene, our mobility test course Toyota Woven City, and Toyota’s growth fund, Woven Capital, we are pioneering the movement of people, goods, information, and energy, weaving a future of enhanced safety, connectivity, and well-being for all," previewed the Japanese automaker on the concept.

Early investors in Woven City include Daikin Industries (OTCPK:DKILF), DyDo DRINCO, Nissin Foods (NPFDF), UCC Japan, and Zoshinkai Holding. A second wave of inventors was made up of Toyota Group companies, including Toyota Industries Corporation (OTCPK:TYIDY), JTEKT Corporation, Toyota Auto Body Co., Toyota Tsusho Corporation, Aisin Corporation, DENSO Corporation, Toyota Boshoku Corporation, Toyota Motor East Japan, Toyoda Gosei Co., and Toyota Motor Kyushu.

Ten ETFs that play into some of the themes of Woven City are the Global X Autonomous & Electric Vehicles ETF (DRIV), the iShares Self-Driving EV and Tech ETF (IDRV), the ARK Autonomous Technology & Robotics ETF (BATS:ARKQ), the Robo Global Robotics & Automation Index ETF (NYSEARCA:ROBO), the First Trust Nasdaq Artificial Intelligence and Robotics ETF (NASDAQ:ROBT), the Fidelity Electric Vehicles and Future Transportation ETF (FDRV), the VanEck Robotics ETF (NASDAQ:IBOT), the Themes Humanoid Robotics ETF (NASDAQ:BOTT), the SPDR S&P Kensho Smart Mobility ETF (HAIL), and the Direxion Daily Electric and Autonomous Vehicles Bull 2X ETF (EVAV).

data.title: SA Asks: How will H-1B visa changes impact the healthcare sector?
data.description:
How will President Trump's proposed changes to the H-1B visa program, which includes hiking fees to $100,000 per visa, impact the healthcare sector?

Seeking Alpha analysts Tunga Capital, Brett Ashcroft Green and Jack Bowman gave us their thoughts.

Tunga Capital: Healthcare constitutes a smaller share of H-1B use, about 6% or 24,000 applications in 2024, but as a sector, it is way more important than tech. Because the policy landed like a blunt instrument, you have this whack-a-mole situation with the government now creating carve-outs for doctors. That explains the price action in HCA (HCA) and Tenet (THC). 

Not everyone in healthcare is a physician, though. Pharmaceutical firms depend on PhD scientists, biologists, chemists, etc., none of whom are covered.

Pharmacos like Amgen (AMGN) and IQVIA (IQV) may begrudge paying ~$10 million in hiring costs. Economist Britta Glennon’s 2023 study found that for every visa rejection, firms hired 0.9 employees abroad. That makes offshoring the most likely path for Big Pharma, with smaller firms exploring other paths like the O-1 visa. Because of the margins, subversions are more likely here than in tech.

Brett Ashcroft Green: Healthcare should run into similar issues as Big Tech. Many talented medical professionals come in on H-1B visas and may be retained by an EB-2/EB-3 workaround. I still do not buy into the idea that these individuals are a cheaper solution, as the ones I’ve spoken to are well compensated; instead, they often fill U.S. labor holes, especially in rural areas. This could raise healthcare costs as staffing shortages ensue. 

Furthermore, the $100,000 H-1B fee could put some medical practices under. We here at Seeking Alpha follow several well-known hospital REITs and know there are several hospital networks on the brink of insolvency. Cost-wise, the $100,000 filing fee is more significant for healthcare.

I also imagine that H-1B filing fees will impact biotech, but not as deeply as the medical industry, particularly hospitals. Biotech researchers may still be able to access uncapped O-1 visas for senior research scientists, whereas hospital employees would rarely gain access to this visa type, which is reserved for individuals with extraordinary abilities in their fields.

Jack Bowman: Biotech and pharma programs that recruit scientists from abroad do so likely because of the lack of local talent, not price, meaning that the increased cost of the new H-1B program will just be a cost to pass on to shareholders of many of these pre-revenue biotechs. The change could shorten cash runways, especially in firms heavily reliant on foreign labor to fill the gaps. 

Biotech will likely be hit the hardest, as those firms are the least able to absorb increased costs and have nowhere else to turn for labor. Increasing the domestic labor supply for biotech and pharma companies is a real problem and won't be solved overnight.

In the meantime, it seems like the firms hit by this change will have little choice but to weather the storm as best they can or lay off those workers if they don't have the cash. There doesn't seem to be a good middle ground for this sector, unlike tech, which can offshore and replace some workers with AI far more easily.

data.title: CSX tie-up with BNSF puts the 'pieces in place' for outperformance to peers - Wells Fargo
data.description:
CSX’s (NASDAQ:CSX) intermodal agreement with Burlington-Northern-Santa Fe (BNSF) is expected to drive demand to CSX’s network that would have interchanged with Norfolk Southern (NSC), putting the “pieces in place” for CSX (NASDAQ:CSX) to outperform its non-aligned peers.

In anticipation of CSX (NASDAQ:CSX) to see strong earnings growth in 2026 as it laps network challenges and coal headwinds, and with tailwinds from the BNSF tie-up, Wells Fargo upgrades the railroad operator to Overweight from Equal Weight and nudges its target price up by 8% to $40.

“We get the sense that the BNSF/CSX commercial agreement is beginning to drive [transcontinental] intermodal business to CSX that would have interchanged with Norfolk Southern,” Wells Fargo analyst Christian Wetherbee said in his note to clients, adding that this relationship is 35% of the $1B transport company J.B. Hunt (JBHT) spends in the east.

In August, CSX (CSX) and BNSF singed an agreement to expand their intermodal partnership that would provide a new service between Phoenix, Arizona and Atlanta, Georgia, aiming to covert over-the-road freight to rail, as well as new international intermodal service between the Port of New York and New Jersey, and Norfolk Virginia, and Kansas City.

The partnership comes amid speculation that the two would merge to compete with the transcontinental line that would be created by the soon-to-be-merged Union Pacific (UNP) and Norfolk Southern (NSC).

And although BNSF executives maintain merger talks with CSX (CSX) have not occurred, Wetherbee believes that the current competitive environment may necessitate further consolidation if the Union Pacific/Norfolk Southern tie up is approved.

To reflect “industry-leading growth,” Wetherbee raised his 2025 EPS estimate to $1.65 from $1.63, 2026 EPS to $1.90 from $1.80 and lifts revenue estimates by 0.4% to $14.18B in 2025 and by 1.8% to $14.78B in 2026.
